title: while Loops
subtitle: Keep going... and going... until you say stop!
goal: Use while loops to repeat code as long as a condition is true, control them with break and continue, and avoid the dreaded infinite loop.

blocks.heading: What is a while loop? 🔁
blocks.html:
A while loop repeats a block of code while something is true. Think of it like a rule: “while it's still raining, keep your umbrella up.” The moment it stops raining, you put it down.

You used for loops to repeat a known number of times. A while loop is for when you don't know how many times yet — you just keep going until a condition changes.

blocks.heading: The counter pattern
blocks.html:
The most common while loop counts up using a variable. It has three parts:

  • Start the counter: i = 0
  • The condition: while i < 5:
  • Change the counter inside: i += 1

That last step is super important — it's what eventually makes the loop stop!

blocks.caption: Counting from 0 to 4 with while
blocks.code:
i = 0
while i < 5:
    print("i is", i)
    i += 1
print("Done!")
blocks.explain: We start i at 0. The loop runs while i < 5. Each time, we print i and then do i += 1 (which means i = i + 1). Once i reaches 5, the condition is False and the loop stops.

blocks.html: i += 1 is a shortcut for i = i + 1. It bumps the counter up by one. You'll see it in almost every while loop!

blocks.caption: A countdown rocket 🚀
blocks.code:
count = 5
while count > 0:
    print(count)
    count -= 1
print("Blast off! 🚀")
blocks.explain: This time we count DOWN. We start at 5 and use count -= 1 to subtract one each loop. When count hits 0, the condition count > 0 is False, so the loop ends and we launch.

blocks.heading: The danger: infinite loops ⚠️
blocks.html:
What if you forget to change the counter? Then the condition never becomes false, and the loop runs forever and ever. This is called an infinite loop, and it freezes your program.

Here is what a broken loop looks like (do NOT run this — it would never stop):

i = 0
while i < 5:
    print(i)
    # oops! we forgot i += 1, so i is always 0

The fix is always the same: make sure something inside the loop moves you closer to making the condition false.

Watch out for infinite loops! Every while loop MUST change the variable in its condition, or it will run forever. Before you run a loop, ask: “What makes this stop?” If you can't answer, fix it first.

blocks.heading: break: stop early 🛑
blocks.html: Sometimes you want to jump out of a loop right away, before the condition naturally becomes false. The word break does exactly that — it breaks out of the loop immediately.

blocks.caption: Guess the secret number (using break)
blocks.code:
secret = 7
guess = 1
while guess <= 10:
    print("Trying", guess)
    if guess == secret:
        print("Found it! The secret was", secret)
        break
    guess += 1
blocks.explain: We pretend to guess numbers 1 through 10. When our guess matches the secret (7), break stops the loop right away — no need to keep checking 8, 9, and 10. (We use a preset secret so it runs the same way every time.)

blocks.heading: continue: skip this turn ⏭️
blocks.html: The word continue skips the rest of the current loop turn and jumps back to the top to check the condition again. It's like saying “skip this one, keep looping.”

blocks.caption: Print only the odd numbers
blocks.explain: We change n FIRST, then check it. If n is even (n % 2 == 0), continue skips the print and goes back to the top. So only odd numbers get printed. Notice we still do n += 1 BEFORE continue, or we'd loop forever!

blocks.heading: for vs while: which one?
blocks.html:
Both loops repeat code, so when do you use each?

  • Use for when you know how many times (looping over a list, or range(5)).
  • Use while when you DON'T know the count — keep going until something happens (a number is guessed, a player wins, the user quits).

Rule of thumb: known count → for, unknown → while.

blocks.question: When does a while loop stop?
options: Only when you press a key
blocks.explain: A while loop keeps running as long as its condition is True, and stops the moment that condition becomes False.

blocks.question: Why is this loop dangerous?  i = 0  /  while i < 3:  /  print(i)
options: i is never changed, so it loops forever (infinite loop)
i starts at the wrong number
blocks.explain: Nothing inside the loop changes i, so i < 3 is always True. That's an infinite loop. Adding i += 1 inside would fix it.

blocks.question: What does break do inside a loop?
options: Stops the loop immediately and moves on
Restarts the loop from the beginning
blocks.explain: break leaves the loop right away. (continue is the one that skips just the current turn.)

blocks.title: Count up to 5
blocks.instructions: This loop counts 1 to 5. The line i += 1 is the magic that makes i grow so the loop knows when to stop. Run it, then try changing 5 to 8 and run again!
blocks.starter:
i = 1
while i <= 5:
    print(i)
    i += 1   # this line moves i forward so the loop ends
blocks.passMsg: Counted to 5 without an infinite loop — well done!
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you have i += 1 inside the loop, and that it prints 1 through 5.

blocks.title: Rocket countdown
blocks.instructions: Almost done! The countdown already shrinks with count -= 1 so it can't run forever. Add a line to print(count) inside the loop so it shows 3, 2, 1 before Liftoff.
blocks.starter:
count = 3
while count > 0:
    # TODO: print(count) here
    count -= 1
print("Liftoff!")
blocks.solution:
count = 3
while count > 0:
    print(count)
    count -= 1
print("Liftoff!")
blocks.passMsg: 🚀 Liftoff! Your countdown loop works perfectly.
blocks.failMsg: Print count, then use count -= 1 inside the loop so it counts down to 0.

blocks.title: Surprise Quiz! Days 6–10
blocks.intro: Pop quiz! Let's see what stuck from the last 5 days. No grades, just fun 🎉

questions.question: Day 6: What does 4 == 4 give you?
questions.explain: == asks 'are these equal?'. 4 really equals 4, so the answer is True.

questions.question: Day 7: With score = 80, which branch runs?  if score >= 90: print("A")  elif score >= 80: print("B")  else: print("C")
questions.explain: 80 is not >= 90 but it IS >= 80, so the first matching branch runs and prints B. Python stops at the first True.

questions.question: Day 8: What is the index of the FIRST item in a list?
questions.explain: Python starts counting at 0, so the first item is always at index 0.

questions.question: Day 9: What numbers does range(1, 4) give you?
questions.explain: range(start, stop) begins at 1 and stops just BEFORE 4 — so you get 1, 2, 3.

questions.question: Day 10: When does a while loop stop?
options: Only when you press a key
questions.explain: A while loop keeps running while its condition is True, and stops the moment that condition becomes False.

challenge.title: The secret-number guesser 🔢
challenge.instructions: The guess goes up by 1 each time (guess += 1 is already there, so it won't loop forever). Your job: inside the loop, if guess == secret, print a 'Found it!' message and use break to stop.
challenge.starter:
secret = 4
guess = 1
while guess <= 10:
    print("Guessing", guess)
    # TODO: if guess == secret -> print "Found it!" and break
    guess += 1
challenge.solution:
secret = 4
guess = 1
while guess <= 10:
    print("Guessing", guess)
    if guess == secret:
        print("Found it! The secret was", secret)
        break
    guess += 1
challenge.hint: Inside the loop: print the guess, check if it equals secret (win + break), and if not, do guess += 1 so you move to the next number.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Cracked the code! You've mastered while loops, break, and counters. Day 10 complete!
